content.full_text:
i.e
Cainornia
Fair Political
Practices Commission
September 18,
1992
Robert E.
Murphy
City Attorney
City of Woodland
Kronick,
Moskovitz,
Tiedernann
& Girard
Attorneys at Law
770
L Street,
Suite
1200
Sacramento,
CA
95814-3363
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-92—559
Dear Mr.
Murphy:
This letter is in response to your request for advice
regarding the responsibilities of Mayor Elaine Rominger and
Councilmember Joe Crescione under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
UEST IONS
1.
May Mayor Rominger and Councilmember Crescione,
each of
whom owns real property located within 2,500 feet of a proposed
development project, participate in decisions concerning the
proj ect?
2.
May Mayor Rominger,
who has received more than
$250 over
the past twelve months from the Yolo County Farm Bureau,
a
nonprofit entity, participate in decisions concerning the Taorrnino
project?
3.
May the mayor participate in the review and update of the
city’s general plan,
where the general plan currently includes
policies intended to geographically limit the city’s urban growth
and discourage the conversion of agricultural land to urban uses?
4.
May the mayor participate in the review and updating of
those portions of the city’s general plan not specifically related
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated:
Corthñission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807
•
Sacramento, CA
95812
•
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-92-559
Page
2
to the expansion of the urbanized portion of the city or the
conversion of agricultural land to urban uses?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
To the extent that the appraiser’s report which you have
submitted for our consideration is correct, the real property
interests of Mayor Rominger and Councilmember Cresciorie are not
disqualifying.
Thus,
absent other disqualifying economic
interests,
they may participate in the pending decisions.
2.
Mayor Rominger may not participate in a governmental
decision where it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect on the Farm Bureau.
3 and 4.
Without more detailed information regarding the
pending general plan decisions and their relationship to the
mayor’s economic interests,
we are unable to provide advice
regarding the proposed general plan amendment.
FACTS
In the near future,
the Woodland City Council may be
considering issues related to the proposed Taormino project.
The
project would consist of 275 single—family homes and
28 acres of
quasi—public facilities on a 164—acre parcel located outside of
both the present boundaries of the City of Woodland and the “urban
limit line.”
Mayor Rominger owns her home located at 703 Rubicon Place
Court.
Her home is beyond 300 feet but within 2,500 of the
proposed development.
Councilmember Crescione owns his home at
1614 Donner Way.
His home is beyond 300 feet but within 2,500
feet of the proposed development.
You have submitted for our
consideration an appraisal performed by Lynch
& Associates which
concludes that the Taormino project may have an effect of
approximately $5,000 on each residence.
2
Mayor Rominger has received in excess of $250 within the
preceding twelve months from her former employer,
the Yolo County
Farm Bureau
(the
“Farm Bureau”).
Mayor Roininger’s spouse is the
president of the Farm Bureau and a member of its board of
directors.
He does not receive compensation for his services to
the Farm Bureau.
2
At your request,
in providing this advice,
we have taken into
consideration a letter from Mr. Alexander Froehlich addressed to
ou and dated June 5,
1992,
and a letter from MayorRominger
addressed to Scott Hallabrin of this office dated August 24,
1992,
both of which you submitted for our consideration.
We note also
that we have received a letter from Mr.
Froehlich addressed to
Scott Hallabrin and dated August 25,
1992,
regarding this matter.


Our File No.
A-92-559
Page
3
The Farm Bureau is a nonprofit mutual benefit corporation
with gross annual receipts of approximately $200,000.
Its
principal asset is a 8.34—acre parcel of real property located
within the City of Woodland.
The needs assessment for the
Taormino project identified and evaluated alternative sites where
a large lot development on the scale proposed by the developer of
the Taormino project could be located.
The Farm Bureau property
is adjacent to the Aoki parcel,
one of several parcels discussed
in the “alternative locations” portion of the needs assessment.
The Farm Bureau property was not considered for the project and is
not the subject of any study or action.
In two previous advice letters ( Advice Letters,
Nos.
A-
90-580 and A—90—757),
we responded to the City of Woodland’s
request for advice on behalf of Mayor Elaine Rominger
3 and
Councilmember Joe Crescione regarding the Taormino project.
We
advised that their real property interests located within 2,500
feet of the proposed Taormino project were not disqualifying
financial interests.
ur conclusioi3 was based on the report of an
appraiser which you submitted for our review.
The appraiser’s
report,
which took into consideration the factors in Regulation
18702.3(d),
concluded that the project would not affect the public
officials’ real property interests in the threshold amounts of
Regulation 18702.3(a) (3) (A)
and (B).
4
ANALYSIS
The Act was adopted by the voters of the State of California
by initiative in 1974.
The purpose for the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act is to ensure that public officials,
whether
elected or appointed,
perform their duties in an impartial manner,
free from any bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them.
(Section
81001(b).)
In furtherance of this goal,
Section 87100 of the Act
provides:
No public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in making
or in any way attempt to use his official position
Mayor Rominger was identified in our previous advice letters by
her former name,
Elaine Timothy.
You have also stated in your request for advice that the city
is considering whether to begin the process of updating the
general plan.
Without more detailed information regarding the
pending general plan decisions and their relation to the mayor’s
economic interests,
we are unable to provide advice regarding the
general plan amendment.
For your convenience,
we are enclosing
our Stone Advice Letter,
No.
1—92—133,
which provides guidance
with respect to general plan amendments.


Our File No.
A-92-559
Page
4
to influence a governmental decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial
interest.
As members of the City Council of the City of Woodland,
Mayor
Rominger and Councilmember Crescione are public officials for
purposes of the Act.
(Section 82048.)
Thus,
they may not make,
participate in making,
or in any way attempt to use their official
position to influence a governmental decision in which they have a
financial interest.
5
Economic Interests
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official,
or on a member of the official’s immediate family,
or
on:
Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth one
thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within
12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
Section 87103(b)
and (c).
Mayor Rominger and Councilmember Crescione own real property
in the City of Woodland.
Presumably, their interests in real
property are greater than $1,000.
Accordingly,
they may not make,
participate in making,
or in any way attempt to use their official
position to influence a governmental decision which will
foreseeably and materially affect their interests in real
property.
In addition,
Mayor Rominger may not participate in a
governmental decision which will foreseeably and materially affect
Please note that absent an economic interest which will be
affected by a governmental decision, the conflict—of—fnterest
provisions of the Act do not apply.
Thus, the personal views or
background of a public official are not of relevance for purposes
of a conflict—of—interest analysis.


Our File No.
A-92-559
Page
5
the Farm Bureau which has been a source of income to her in excess
of $250 in the preceding twelve months.
6
Whether the financial effect of a decision is reasonably
foreseeable at the time the governmental decision is made depends
on the facts of each particular case.
An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (
re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)
It is foreseeable that a development such as the Taormino
project, consisting of 275 single-family units and 28 acres of
quasi—public facilities on a 164-acre parcel, will have an effect
on the value of real property located in the vicinity of the
project.
If the effect will be material, disqualification is
required.
Real Property Interests
As we have previously advised with regard to the real
property interests of Mayor Rominger and Councilrnember Crescione,
the appropriate standard for determining materiality when real
property will be affected indirectly by a governmental decision is
that of Regulation 18702.3(a) (3) (A)
and
(B).
This regulation
states that a decision is material if the decision will have an
effect of $10,000 or more on the fair market value of the real
property or will have an effect of $1,000 or more per twelve month
period in the rental value of the real property.
(IiQ Advice
Letter,
No.
A-90-757.)
You have submitted for our consideration an appraisal that
states that the pending decisions will affect the officials’
interests in real property by approximately $5,000.
In reaching
this conclusion,
the appraiser has taken into consideration the
factors in Regulation 18702.3(d).
Accordingly,
to the extent that
the appraiser’s conclusions are accurate,
7 the officials’
interests in real property are not disqualifying economic
6
Mayor Rominger’s spouse,
Mr.
Rominger,
is the president of the
Farm Bureau and a member of the board of directors.
Section 82030
provides that the income of an individual also includes any
community property interest in the income of a spouse.
(Newman
Advice Letter,
No.
A—91-515.)
However,
your facts indicate that
the Farm Bureau is not a source of income to Mayor Rominger’s
spouse.
Thus,
Mr.
Rominger’s activities on behalf of the Farm
Bureau are not a disqualifying economic interest.
-
The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it issues
its advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
Accordingly,
the advice provided in this letter is applicable only to the
extent that the facts provided in your request for advice are
correct and all material facts have been presented.


Our File No.
A-92--559
Page
6
interests and they may participate in the pending decisions unless
other economic interests would require their disqualification.
Source of Income
You have also stated in your request for advice that Mayor
Rominger,
formerly an employee of the Farm Bureau,
has received
income from the Farm Bureau in excess of $250 in the preceding
twelve months.
Thus, the Farm Bureau is a potentially
disqualifying economic interest.
The appropriate standard for determining materiality with
respect to a nonprofit entity which is a source of income to a
public official is that of Regulation 18702.5.
With respect to an
entity such as the
Farm Bureau which has gross annual receipts of
approximately $200,000, disqualification is required if:
(1)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease of the entity’s grass annual receipts
for a fiscal year in the amount of $50,000 or more.
(2)
The decision will cause the entity to
incur or avoid additional expenses or to reduce or
eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal year in
the amount of $12,500 or more.
(3)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the entity’s assets or
liabilities in the amount of $50,000 or more.
Thus, if a governmental decision will affect the Farm Bureau in
the above sums,
Mayor Rorninger’s disqualification will be
required.
The Farm Bureau is a nonprofit entity which owns land within
the City of Woodland.
It would appear that a parcel situated
adjacent to the Farm Bureau’s land has been considered as an
alternative site for development.
We have previously advised that
the general rule is that every decision must be analyzed
independently to determine the existence of a foreseeable material
effect on an official’s economic interests.
(In re Owen
(1976)
2
FPPC Ops.
77; Kilian Advice Letter,
No.
A-89-522; Huffaker Advice
Letter,
No.
A—86—343.)
However,
in some cases, decisions may be
too interrelated to be considered separately.
If the resolution
of one decision will effectively determine the result of the other
decision,
a public official is disqualified as to both. (
Advice Letter,
No.
1—91-436.)
In the Abt advice,
we stated:
1•
.if a decision is to place
new building on one of two locations,
the decision to place the
building at one location necessarily decides whether the building
is to be placed at the alternate location.
If an official has a
financial interest in the alternate location,
he may not
participate in the decision.”
Thus, if decisions regarding the


Our File No.
A-92-559
Page
7
Taormino project are related to whether development will proceed
in the Aoki property which is located immediately adjacent to the
Farm Bureau property,
the standards of Regulation 18702.5 should
be applied to determine whether the decisions will have a material
effect on the Farm Bureau.
If the effect will be material,
Mayor
Rominger’s disqualification will be required.
Nexus
In addition,
a public official with a disqualifying economic
interest may not participate in a governmental decision if there
is a “nexus.”
A “nexus” exists if an official receives income in
his or her private capacity to achieve a goal or purpose which
would be achieved, defeated,
aided,
or hindered by the
governmental decision.
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (1); Richards Advice
Letter,
No.
1—92—024.)
The rationale underlying the “nexus” test
is that when a connection exists between a person’s job and his or
her role as a public official, there is a presumption that the
value of the employee’s services tothe employer is based at least
in part on the fact that the employee is a public official.
(Richards,
supra.)
However,
because Mayor Rominger is no longer
an employee of the Farm Bureau, the “nexus” test is not applicable
in this instance.
We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter,
do
not hesitate to call me at
(916)
322_5901.8
Sincerely,
SCOTT HALLABRIN
Acting General Counsel
By:
Blanca M.
Breeze
Counsel,
Legal Division
SH:BMB:aa
8
Copies of Commission regulations and opinions ar available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of
1OQ per page.

have a material financial effect on the Farm Bureau.
3 and 4.
Without more detailed information regarding the
pending general plan decisions and their relationship to the
mayor’s economic interests,
we are unable to provide advice
regarding the proposed general plan amendment.

No.

However,
because Mayor Rominger is no longer
an employee of the Farm Bureau, the “nexus” test is not applicable
in this instance.
We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.

2.

embedding.first_500_words: i.e Cainornia Fair Political Practices Commission September 18, 1992 Robert E. Murphy City Attorney City of Woodland Kronick, Moskovitz, Tiedernann & Girard Attorneys at Law 770 L Street, Suite 1200 Sacramento, CA 95814-3363 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-92—559 Dear Mr. Murphy: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the responsibilities of Mayor Elaine Rominger and Councilmember Joe Crescione under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”). 1 UEST IONS 1. May Mayor Rominger and Councilmember Crescione, each of whom owns real property located within 2,500 feet of a proposed development project, participate in decisions concerning the proj ect? 2. May Mayor Rominger, who has received more than $250 over the past twelve months from the Yolo County Farm Bureau, a nonprofit entity, participate in decisions concerning the Taorrnino project? 3. May the mayor participate in the review and update of the city’s general plan, where the general plan currently includes policies intended to geographically limit the city’s urban growth and discourage the conversion of agricultural land to urban uses? 4. May the mayor participate in the review and updating of those portions of the city’s general plan not specifically related Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated: Corthñission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, . All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. A-92-559 Page 2 to the expansion of the urbanized portion of the city or the conversion of agricultural land to urban uses? CONCLUSIONS 1. To the extent that the appraiser’s report which you have submitted for our consideration is correct, the real property interests of Mayor Rominger and Councilmember Cresciorie are not disqualifying. Thus, absent other disqualifying economic interests, they may participate in the pending decisions. 2. Mayor Rominger may not participate in a governmental decision where it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the Farm Bureau. 3 and 4. Without more detailed information regarding the pending general plan decisions and their relationship to the mayor’s economic interests, we are unable to provide advice regarding the proposed general plan amendment. FACTS In the near future, the Woodland City Council may be considering issues related to the proposed Taormino project. The project would consist of 275 single—family homes and 28 acres of quasi—public facilities on a 164—acre parcel located outside of both the present boundaries of the City of Woodland and the “urban limit line.” Mayor Rominger owns her home located at 703 Rubicon Place Court. Her home is beyond 300 feet but within 2,500 of the proposed development. Councilmember Crescione owns his home at 1614 Donner Way. His home is beyond 300 feet but within 2,500 feet of the proposed development. You have submitted for our consideration an appraisal performed
